Transcrição Completa: B2B Sales Foundation Class - Dan Lok Organization
[00:00] All right. Hey, good afternoon, CITs, as well as any IC members watching this in the future. So what we're doing today, I just want to welcome you and thank you for jumping on with your time. We're going to cover B2B, basically a foundation class, right? It's not really a training. It's more on really the foundations, the basics of what you got to know in order to be successful at B2B sales.
[00:25] The reason we came up with this class is because we realized that there were a lot of IC members and HTC brothers, sisters, family members who wanted to do B2B sales, but might not or did not really have the background experience or even the business acumen in knowing how to really close one of these deals or do these discovery calls or do lead gen properly, right?
[00:48] So this class today is meant to, it's not meant to be like the entire know-all, be-all, but it's meant to give you a good enough foundation that you would be able to take this and use it for yourself or with us in the B2B sales division, right?
[01:05] So I'm going to share my screen and a couple of slides and we'll kick off. And also today we have the B2B sales division manager, Sean, as well as the sales support manager, Felix, here with me. And we are going to kind of walk you through this journey.
[01:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Sales Foundation Class"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados neste momento
VISUAL: Slide de abertura da apresentação, título principal destacado, layout profissional corporativo
[01:35] Perfect. Okay. And let me know in the chat if you guys can see my screen. So just confirm because I don't see anyone in the chat right now. Perfect. Okay. So it's like I said, it's a foundation, okay?

NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Lista negativa destacando o que a aula não será, formato de bullet points

That's the worst thing that can happen is when, you know, you come on like a webinar or whatever and then just PowerPoint hitting you, right? I want it to be conversational. I want it to be interactive. I want you guys to ask questions.
[02:10] But please, you know, before just asking questions, raise your hand. And I think, Jason, if you could help tell me, because I don't look at a chat, you just help tell me when there's a hand up, you can unmute yourself and just tell us. Or maybe Felix or Sean can help as well.
[02:25] This is not a UHTC class, right? This is not a replacement for ultra UHTC. You're going to get a lot of value. You're going to learn a lot from ultra UHTC. But that is only in, right now it's in module two, correct? So we still have a long ways to go.
[02:42] And the B2B division, we don't want to wait till the end of ultra UHTC to find good closers. We need good closers like yesterday. And this is why we have this today as well.
[02:54] And it is not the A to Z of B2B. Like I said, it's not the know-all, be-all. It's not everything. We're not going to be able to cover everything you got to know. That takes years of experience, right? And there are people who are way more also qualified to do this other than myself, right?
[03:10] So you will learn through your experiences and you learn through your own journey. But we want you to have this foundation. Like think of a house building a building. You get that basic foundation. So at least in the future when you learn more things, when you go to ultra UHTC class, a lot of this will make more sense to you, okay?

[04:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "1. B2B Sales Talk (Lingo/Jargon)"
NÚMEROS: Ponto #1
VISUAL: Primeira seção destacada, possivelmente com ícones de diálogo ou comunicação profissional
[04:12] So one of it is the B2B sales talk. That's lingo. That's like the jargon. That's like, you know, we'll go over some examples and you'll see what I mean as well. We'll give you guys some role-play examples as well as some statements that you could use.
[04:26] And you will see why it's so important to have that business acumen. Really, that first point is business acumen, right? The difference between a B2C closer and a B2B closer is that a B2B closer has a specific set of jargon, lingo, basically vocabulary, right? That they have to use in terms to sound more professional, to have that business acumen, right?
[04:48] B2C is a lot more personable. It's a lot more one-on-one, right? It's a lot more, I can talk about your emotions a lot more. It's more cash. It should be more casual. It should be more colloquial, right? B2B is definitely different.
[05:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "2. Posturing, Framing & Confidence"
NÚMEROS: Ponto #2
VISUAL: Segunda seção com ênfase em postura profissional, possivelmente ícones de apresentação ou linguagem corporal
[05:07] The second thing is then the posturing, the framing, the confidence. Something that we found, which is what Felix brought up recently, which is very, very important, is he's noticed and we've noticed that some people, they know what to say, they know what questions to ask, but they don't have that proper frame, that proper confidence on a call, even on a discovery call or a closing call, that business owners and executives and directors, they just shrug them off because they don't respect them, right?
[05:38] They don't have that. Your tonality is off. Your framing is off. You speak too fast. You speak too slow. You don't answer the questions the way the executive expected to answer it. He or she thinks you're beating around the bush when you're not. It's like these kind of things that really make or break a deal, right?
[05:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3. Crucial B2B Questions to Ask"
NÚMEROS: Ponto #3

[06:00] And then crucial B2B questions to ask. So these are the things that when you ask these questions, right, it will lead you to a higher chance of success, getting an appointment, getting a sale. These are things that you almost never want to not ask. They have to be asked, really.
[06:18] So this is like, think of it like a framework or a cheat sheet, if you will, to help you in your B2B journey, okay?
[06:26] And then by the end of this class today, right, you're going to be able to ask a lot of questions, have your questions answered, and then you will know, right, is this for me? But maybe it's not for me, right? And this will help you also clarify what you want to do, where you want to go in your journey as well.

[10:05] JOEL (continuando sobre terminologia): ...is like an e-book in B2C. So a white paper is a paper written on a particular business topic, normally again with a sales intended focus to be able to give person information about a particular product or service, how it would benefit their business, how it would, sometimes it has some case studies in it from people that have used the product or service.
[10:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "White Paper"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Definição destacada do termo "White Paper", comparação visual com e-book B2C
[10:30] So a little bit different than an e-book on the B2C side, but that's what a white paper is.
[10:36] Exactly. Make sure you write that down as well. I should have added it to my slide. Yeah, but like Sean said, it's really much like a lead magnet, like an e-book on the B2C side. They just say white paper because in B2B, it's actually what it was in the past. Back in the old days, they actually sent out those papers to their MQLs, their marked direct mail. So that's why it's white paper.
[10:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NSAs - Non-Sales Related Activities"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Definição e exemplos de atividades não relacionadas a vendas
[11:00] The next one, NSAs, non-sales related activities. So just think about it, right? You're a closer, right? When you do prospecting, when you follow up emails, when you do calls, when you do CRM stuff, those are all sales related activities.
[11:16] It's the things like when you go and fill out your commission report, when you go and talk to your manager, when you go and talk to the product manager team, like Felix's team, then that's not really a sales related activity, right? That's an NSA, right?
[11:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MQL & SQL Clarification"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Distinção visual entre Marketing Qualified Lead e Sales Qualified Lead, possivelmente com funil de vendas
[11:32] FELIX: One thing I want to add on to the MQL and SQL a little bit. I had a little bit of experience myself when I was working in a startup environment where the product team that I have also worked close with marketing. So just want to clarify a little bit because different companies treat MQL's definition differently, right?
[11:52] They might think enrolling in an email sequence in MQL. Some company like mine, like the one that I work with, they see they need to do multiple actions to become an MQL. So they might need to download PDF, they might need to subscribe to the email and talk to someone before they become an MQL.
[12:08] So just want to make sure that you guys are not assuming, you know, subscribing email directly equals to MQL. It varies, depends on the company. So when you guys are talking to business owners, you know, you don't want to make that assumption. That's what I'm trying to say. Definitely clarify that.
[12:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lead Score"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de pontuação de leads, possivelmente com escala ou sistema de pontos
[12:27] JOEL: The other term I would add, if you can write it down, is lead score. Usually companies determine their MQLs based on the lead score. Like Felix said in his organization, right? If a lead comes in, maybe they only clicked on an email or they only replied to an email, that score is not enough to become an MQL. Then that's not an MQL, right?
[12:48] So like Felix is saying, the company might calculate the number of scores, things they do, the actions they do. Click on an email, download an ebook, right? You know, click on an ad, comment it somewhere, right? On a blog post. Then those scores add up. Then they might become an MQL. So exactly right with what Felix is saying there.
[13:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Revenue Terminology - Critical Section"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado neste momento
VISUAL: Seção destacada sobre diferentes tipos de receita, possivelmente com ícones de dinheiro ou gráficos financeiros
[13:12] Okay, these ones are much more important because when I found these very important when it comes to talking to business owners about revenue, money. I think one of my mistakes, the biggest mistakes I have made on B2B when I first started was not knowing how to, not knowing, well, first of all, I didn't really know the difference between the types of revenue.
[13:35] That's a lot of type of revenue, right? Like that's front end, back end, deferred, total, collected, analyzed. That's all kinds of different terms. I'm not saying today go learn all the accounting terms or the financial terms, but I will tell you this, a lot of different business owners, they can use very different terms and very conflicting terms to actually define the same thing.
[14:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Annual Revenue vs Annual Contract Value"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo de $10M mencionado
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre diferentes definições de receita, possivelmente com exemplos lado a lado
[14:02] But I talked to one guy, I talked to one business owner, he said, my annual revenue, right, was X, okay? And then that to him, okay, let's say my annual revenue last year was 10 mil, okay? Then to me, that means you made last year, calendar year, by the way, we're gonna talk about fiscal year and calendar year as well.
[14:24] For me, it means in my head, last calendar year, your company did $10 million, right, front end in revenue, or front end back in everything in revenue, right? But then someone else might say, my revenue last year was 10 mil, right, or something like that.
[14:40] Or sometimes I've heard the term cash flow, right? Or our, and sometimes it'll be very direct, right? If you ask them about profits, they actually know what it is, but it's important to know the difference between all these terms.
[14:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SaaS Revenue Models - ACV Example"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado diretamente
VISUAL: Exemplo de modelo de receita SaaS, foco em Annual Contract Value
[14:57] So like, if you talk to a SaaS company or you talk to a company that's a lot of subscriptions, they might not even say revenue, they might just tell you about, talk about annual contract values all day long.
[15:10] For example, Grant Cardone's, Cardone University, his team, when I talk to them, they only talk about ACVs, annual contract value. They don't even talk about revenue. When you ask them, what's your revenue last year? They go, do you mean our annual contract values? I go, oh, okay, yes.
[15:28] Because they're talking about what is each account's contract value worth that year, right, and they add it up. So annual contract value per account, right? But then when they understand what I'm saying, then they go, oh, okay, got it. So you just mean the bulk of it or the total. Yeah, that's what I mean by the revenue. Got it, okay.
[15:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MRR - Monthly Recurring Revenue"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo de $250/mês mencionado
VISUAL: Definição de MRR com exemplo prático do Inner Circle
[15:50] Monthly recurring revenue is how much monthly recurring subscriptions are. So for example, like I think a perfect example is IC in the circle. If you're paying 250 a month, then that's 250 a month recurring revenue, right, from that one client. That's monthly recurring. It goes on and on.
[16:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ARPA - Average Revenue Per Account"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Definição de ARPA, possivelmente com exemplo de cálculo médio
[16:12] The ARPA is the average MRR per account. So if we had HTC, right, the HTC brand, that's the ARPA there. We have HIC, that's the other MRR there, right? It's two different divisions, right? Okay, we're good.
[16:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ARR - Annual Recurring Revenue"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Definição de ARR, distinção de receita recorrente anual
[16:30] And then the annual recurring revenue, again. So again, right, sometimes they might say, hey, my company's annual recurring revenue, that should give you a hint that the company might be doing more subscription-based type products or services.
[16:45] Then I might ask them, so just to clarify, Mr. Prospect, Mr. Business Owner, what you're selling is an annual subscription, correct? They go, oh yeah, that's right. Then I say, oh, okay, got it, right?
[17:00] Because an annual recurring revenue is different from annual revenue in that recurring, it's something they expect to come year after year after year after year. So it's these kind of things, right, to be aware of. Sean and Felix, anything you want to add to this?
[17:18] SEAN: So these terms, the biggest importance of these is this is normally what is really important to your business owner when you're talking to them. So this is one you need to pay a lot of attention as to what and ask the right questions to find out which one or all of these things are important to them, depending on their business model, their revenue model.
[17:40] So this is the place where you're doing a lot of B2B sales, you're going to be asking a lot of questions and you need to make sure it's about what is important to your prospect, not necessarily what's important to you. So make sure you spend the proper time and ask the proper questions about these particular points.
[18:00] JOEL: My biggest mistake to finish off what I was saying earlier was that I only used the term revenue when I first started, and that really messed me up. Because when I was talking to SaaS owners, they could tell I didn't know what I was doing because I just said revenue.
[18:18] When they said recurring revenue or they said annual contract value, I'm like, I was like, I don't know what that is. I had to go Google it, right? So it's things like this where like Sean said, you've got to really got to know what they say and what it means.
[18:35] And if you don't know, you can clarify. So when you say annual recurring revenue, I just want to clarify, you're tying that to your service-based subscription product, service-based subscription, correct? That's what you mean.
[18:50] But then annual revenue for, okay. So your annual recurring revenue is 5 mil, but your annual revenue is 10 mil. Am I correct? So that means 5 mil came from your software service, your SaaS. Got it. Okay. That makes sense, right? It's like, it's those kinds of conversation that you have to have.
[19:10] All right. Anything else? Any questions in the chat? There's a question. Let's see. ACV, MRR, ARR are one of a predictable revenue. Am I right?
[19:25] Now, see, that's what we want to, I want to clarify terms. Shang, when you say predictable, what do you mean? Are you saying more of a forecast? Are you talking about forecast? Like a prediction of revenue? Or are you talking about like the actual collected revenue? We're also got to be on the same page with the terms, right? NÚMEROS: Exemplo: homens 18-35 anos
VISUAL: Definição de perfil de cliente ideal com categorias demográficas, geográficas e psicográficas
[20:48] JOEL: ...the business owner. I might go, okay, so you know, we've got these closers and we want to know what kind of ICP, so ideal customer profiles, your company mainly get or mainly targets.
[21:05] What I mean by that is exactly what I just said, right? What that business owner I'm talking to, his target audience, what is his ideal type? Their demographics, right? Their age, maybe males, 18 to 35, located in North America.
[21:22] So demographic is what the features are, like the person's features, like age, gender, that's demographic, right? Geographic is location. Psychographic is attitude, buying behaviors, right? Things that they do, activities they do, right?
[21:40] So it could be male, 18 to 35, located in Iowa, in the United States, who, just think about like the Facebook targeting. If you guys know Facebook targeting, it's like, then you can select activities, right? And target it based on people who gamble at least once a month. People who, I don't know, like you just, that's what psychographics are, right? Pretty much. And then you want to know what the ICPs are.
[22:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "KPI - Key Performance Indicators"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Definição de KPI com exemplos de métricas de sucesso
[22:08] KPI, I think you've heard of this one as well. It's common in many industries, in many different settings. It's basically, what are your measures of success within your company, right? For that specific task or that specific role.
[22:25] So I might ask the business owner, you know, Mr. Business Owner, what is a key KPI that you expect from one of our closers? And he might tell me, surprisingly actually, you know, Joel, it's not the conversion rate. I actually care more about their follow-up time or the time it takes for them to reach out to a prospect.
[22:48] And I go, oh, so that's an indicator, that's a performance indicator that you measure, right? That you hold highly in regard. They go, yeah, I care about the time it takes to follow up with a client more than a conversion rate. But a conversion rate is a KPI as well, right?
[23:08] Do I see a question? I think Sean and Felix, you're going to help me answer the questions.
[23:15] SEAN: Yeah, well, just before I answer Henry's question there, the ideal customer profile, the other thing you'll hear it said is called an avatar. So if you hear the term avatar, they're talking about the same thing.
[23:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Avatar = ICP"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Equivalência entre termos Avatar e Ideal Customer Profile
[23:35] Henry, as far as an OKR, is it also a common term in certain circles? It is. In certain circles, it's not. KPI is the more prevalent one, but you're seeing OKRs coming out more.
[23:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "OKR - Objectives and Key Results"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Definição de OKR, origem em empresas tech como Google e Intel
[23:52] For those that don't know what an OKR is, it's objective and key results. It was bigger in tech sectors to begin with, but it's now become part of a much broader range of companies. It originally started back with companies like Google and Intel, but it's spread to be a much broader base. Even Bill Gates uses it for his not-for-profit foundation. So it has a lot of broad uses.
[24:18] JOEL: Yeah, we used to use KPIs in Team Dan Lok, and now we are all in with OKR. So if you want to read a book, it's the OKR book by John Dewar. D-O-O-E-R. That's a good book.
[24:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Margins - Profitability"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de margens, lucro vs perda, break-even
[24:38] And the last one, I think margins. I think some of you guys said in the chat just now, margins. It's basically profitability, how much of it, how much of the revenue could be profit. What's the loss? What's the margin between the loss and the profits, and what's break-even? It's how much they make, how much they take home at the end of the day.
[25:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MoM, WoW, QoQ - Time Period Comparisons"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Definições de Month over Month, Week over Week, Quarter over Quarter
[25:02] Cool. All right. These ones are pretty important too. Month over month, week over week, or quarter over quarter. I could say my month over month revenue, my week over week revenue, right? My quarter over quarter revenue. So that's week to week, month over month, right? You kind of average it out, then that's what you get.
[25:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NPS - Net Promoter Score"
NÚMEROS: Escala 0-10, Dan Lok org tem score de 9/10
VISUAL: Sistema de pontuação de satisfação do cliente, escala visual de 0 a 10
[25:28] And then net promoter score, that's something you are very familiar with in terms, you might be familiar with if you come from corporations that do a lot of good client success, customer service, customer satisfaction type surveys, right?
[25:45] It's zero out of 10. How likely are you to recommend your business to others? I'm sure most of us have seen it with, even with the Dan Lok support team. They used to send out the emails on the bottom. You would select zero to 10, right? How likely are you to recommend Dan Lok to others? You guys have seen that email? Yes? No?
[26:08] Yeah, that's a net promoter score. So just to share, our organization score is pretty high, and we're very proud of it. We're like a nine, right? Nine out of 10. That's a very high average. So that's great.
[26:25] So something that, you know, if you talk to a business owner, you might ask them, right? How do you measure, how do you guys measure your client's satisfaction? How do you ensure that your clients are happy? And they might say, oh, we got a net promoter score system.
[26:42] Then that should tell you, got it. So they're using this average, right? Then I might ask them, okay, don't mind me asking, right? Mr. Business Owner, what's the current average right now? It's a seven? And you said you'd like to get it to a nine, correct? Okay, got it.
[27:00] So Mr. Prospect, if our closers could help you get to a eight and a half to begin with for the next 30, 60 days, would that be pretty important? You'd go, yeah, that's fantastic. If they could help me increase my, you know, if they had that great of a client success, client-facing view, sure, I love that, right? Right?
[27:22] Cool. Any questions? Ooh, that was pretty much it. Nice. Any questions on that?
[27:30] Henry just asked if everything over a seven considered as good, what is the average there for a net promoter score?
[27:38] SEAN: Depends on the industry. I will tell you that. Every industry you look at, there's a different average. Automotive, legal, digital marketing, you can Google it. There's very different averages out there.
[27:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NPS Varies by Industry"
NÚMEROS: Score 7-9 mencionados como referência
VISUAL: Comparação de NPS médio por indústria, exemplos de diferentes setores
[27:58] Depends on what industry you're talking about, right? Some services and some industries tend to have higher complaints, or I would say higher risk than others, right? They tend to have a bit lower NPS score or NPS. So the average would depend on the industry, I would say.
[28:18] For something like us, like selling educational products in full type stuff, I mean, again, our NPS for Dan Lok organization is only for HTC HXC. But we don't have an NPS for Dragon 100, right? Yet or whatever.
[28:35] So my educational product, nine is pretty high. I would say nine is pretty damn high. I would say honestly seven is like, okay, this is just my personal opinion, right? Anything under eight to me, I'd be a bit wary of, right? Like if anything under eight, I'd be like, yeah, like, I don't know. But again, it depends on industry.
[28:58] Airlines have a very wide spectrum of NPS scores. Budget airlines, premium airlines, right? Depends if you fly coach, first class, business class, like everyone's going to rate it very differently.
[29:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Posturing, Framing & Confidence - Role Play Section"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Transição para seção de role-play, ênfase em tonalidade e postura profissional
[29:18] Awesome. So I'm going to get some water, Felix and Sean. You guys want to kick this part off and I'll be right back. We're going to do some discussion role plays. And I think this is a really key part in terms of now you know these terms and later you're going to learn some of the very critical questions to ask.
[29:38] But here we're going to talk about the tone, what you're sounding like on the call, right? Like how your pace, tonality, everything. So I'm going to get some water. If Sean and Felix, you guys want to kick this off, I'll be right back.
[29:52] SEAN: Yeah, I'll take it from here, yeah. So I talk to people a lot about this because positioning, framing, and confidence in a B2B call is very important. So it's very different than a B2C call.
[30:08] A B2C call is a lot more personal. You know, you're looking at someone's personal pain versus someone's business pain. The questions you're going to ask in a B2C call are different than the ones you'd ask in a B2B call.
[30:25] But it's also how you present yourself. In a business call, people can see the moment you show any bit of wavering in your frame and confidence. If you come across as you're pretty new and you don't know what you're asking, you're going to have a tough time on B2B calls.
[30:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Confidence Indicators - Verbal Ticks"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Exemplos de sinais de falta de confiança, verbal ticks como "ah", "um", tonalidade hesitante
[30:48] So I'll give you an example. If you come on the call and you go, well, Mr. Prospect, I'm not sure what it is that your business does, but I have something that might be able to help you. Can I ask you about it?
[31:05] You hear the verbal tick of the ah, over and over and over again. It's a measure of confidence. A keen business owner on the other end of the line is going to pick that up and realize you don't know what you're doing or what you're talking about.
[31:22] Business owners are very quick to pick up on that. Business owners also, because they're very busy and you're potentially some days the 14th or 15th person that they've heard from in a day, you have to set yourself apart from the 14 or 15 that called earlier in the day.
[31:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bad Tonality Example"
NÚMEROS: 14-15 chamadas por dia mencionadas
VISUAL: Exemplo de tonalidade condescendente vs profissional, comparação lado a lado
[31:45] A good example I always give is, again, I worked in the IT sector for many years and I dealt with a large supplier. I knew every day that that large supplier was calling because every time they called, their tonality was, oh, hey, how are you? Would you like me to help you? Would you like me to pat your head?
[32:08] (Sean demonstra tonalidade condescendente)
[32:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SDR Role Play - Felix & Sean"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Início de role-play demonstrativo, setup de cenário B2B
[32:20] SEAN: (Iniciando role-play como SDR) How you guys are doing in the face of our current situation, but how are things going for you right now?
[32:32] FELIX: (Como prospect) Well, you know, we have to kind of downsize a little bit on our company because of what's happened two months ago. But yeah, we're surviving. We're changing the way we work to adapt to what's going on with the economy. So yeah, we're doing okay. We're coming up with a lot of ideas, a lot of new plans to implement.
[32:55] SEAN: That sounds good. Can I ask you, have you ever made use of an advisory board before?
[33:02] FELIX: Not really. I mean, I have been talking to a lot of other business owners that do similar things that where I usually get a lot of advice and I taught my own some programs myself as well.
[33:18] SEAN: Okay. And how's that been working for you so far?
[33:22] FELIX: I mean, you know, there's so much information out there. There's just so many different people doing this, doing that. So really finding that one thing that really works for me, it's something that it's, you know, getting there. Just always need to know who to choose from, right?
[33:42] SEAN: Okay. Well, unfortunately, I don't have a lot of time and I'm sure you're a pretty busy person yourself, but let's suppose I had an option available to you where this advisory board would have a group of people who might be able to provide you with some guidance and some advice from trusted sources that have built, you know, seven and eight figure companies that potentially could save you some time and some headache that you've been through in the past.
[34:15] Would that be something of value to you that we could have maybe a deeper discussion on a day where we both have some time?
[34:22] FELIX: How long would that call? What is that call about?
[34:28] SEAN: So the call would take about 15 minutes. I could go into a little more depth about what the advisory board is about. I'd have to ask some questions to make sure that this is a good fit because, you know, this isn't for everybody.
[34:45] So there would be some qualifying questions I need to ask about your business just to make sure that there's value that we can provide to you and you can get value from us. And then we would see where we go from there. Could we do that?
[35:02] FELIX: Okay.
[35:05] SEAN: Okay. Are you going to send me some information? I've never heard of you before, as you said, right?
[35:12] FELIX: For sure. Yeah. I have a document I can send you ahead of time that you can review. And then I can go over that information with you in detail when we connect. Does that sound good?
[35:25] SEAN: Sure.
[35:28] FELIX: Okay. Can I get your proper email address?
[35:32] SEAN: It's felix at mobileapp.com.
[35:38] FELIX: Okay. Felix spelled F-E-L-I-X?
[35:42] SEAN: That's correct.
[35:45] FELIX: Okay. Perfect. And so when would be a good time we could schedule to follow up and I could go through this information with you?
[35:55] SEAN: Well, I'm looking at, I mean, I have Friday at 11 a.m. I can have like 15, 30 minutes there.
[36:05] FELIX: Okay. And you're Pacific time, correct?
[36:08] SEAN: Yes.
[36:10] FELIX: Okay. So 11 a.m. Pacific time on Friday?
[36:15] SEAN: Sure.
[36:18] FELIX: And this is still the best number to get a hold of you at on Friday?
[36:22] SEAN: Yep. What's your name again?
[36:25] FELIX: Sean.
[36:28] SEAN: Sean. Got it. Okay. Am I expecting an email from you or anything like that?
[36:35] FELIX: Yeah. You'll see an email from me. It'll come from seanlawrence at dragon100.com. And if you don't see it right away, just double check your spam folder to make sure it doesn't get caught there. If for some reason there's any problems when we speak on Friday, I can make sure you have that information in your hands when we speak.
[36:58] SEAN: Sounds good.
[37:02] FELIX: Okay. So I look forward to speaking with you at 11 o'clock on Friday and we can get into a little bit deeper dive on how this will benefit your business.
[37:12] SEAN: Cool.
[37:15] FELIX: Awesome. Thank you, Sean.
[37:18] SEAN: Thanks very much.
[37:20] FELIX: Okay. Bye.
[37:22] SEAN: Bye.

[38:15] With what I learned with speaking business owners, they really do prefer to organize a time there and then whether it is on a call or say a message, say LinkedIn or anything like that.
[38:30] SEAN: Yeah, we've actually found in our experience that sending out a booking link is not a great way to make sure you get a return phone call. The best way is to book with them right there.
[38:45] PATRICK: Yeah, actually, this is Patrick. That's actually also what I experienced because I always had to deal with those email brush offs to call it in that way. And I really like the way Sean, you got the commitment in the end. You just ask when is a good time to follow up with you so we could go over that together.
[39:08] And I've even gone to the point where they're like, well, just send me the information and I'll get back to you. I'd rather say, you know what, can I call you tomorrow and just make sure that you got the information? Like I want a commitment out of them one way or the other, because if they're not ready to commit to you, they're probably just blowing you off.
[39:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Getting Commitment - Language Nuance"
