[39:58] And that's just hitting on a little bit more of the emerald, but that's something that he's found a lot of success with as well.
[40:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Crucial B2B Questions to Ask"
NÚMEROS: 4 categorias principais
VISUAL: Seção principal dividida em 4 categorias: Needs, Budget, Decision Making, Closing
[40:10] All right, cool. Let's go on. We are at 5.07. I think we will round this up pretty nicely because we also want to have a couple minutes at the end for Q&As. So let us go into the next section, which is really critical or crucial B2B questions to ask.
[40:28] Again, right? Like just because we say it's crucial or critical doesn't mean in every call you will script it out and ask, like go down the list and ask, right? It really does depend. Use common sense on the flow of the call, the flow, right? That'd be like water.
[40:48] And then when it makes sense to you, then you ask. But we found in our experience that when you don't ask these questions, they usually leave a lot of objections or a lot of gaps in the prospect's mind.
[41:05] And when you do ask these questions properly, it also gives you a bigger, well, better understanding of the business, as well as it helps you formulate next steps, next action plans, as well as understand the different pains and the different goals to hit, right? In terms of setting up the next steps and solution.
[41:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "4 Categories of B2B Questions"
Needs
Budget
Decision Making
Closing
NÚMEROS: 4 categorias
VISUAL: Framework organizado em 4 blocos principais, estrutura clara e hierárquica
[41:32] So it's really simple. I just classify it in like a couple of different steps, right? You got the needs, you got the budget, you got decision making, and you got closing. It's pretty much almost like B2C, but I just put it in those four categories to make it easier to remember, right?
[41:50] Because every time you go on a call, you're always going to talk about those things anyways, right? Those same things.
[41:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NEEDS - Question #1: How did you hear about us?"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Primeira pergunta da categoria Needs destacada
[42:00] So the first one is very simple. It's literally, you know, this is where they're calling you sort of inbound, where they're reaching out to you. You should ask them on a call, hey, you know, Mr. Business Owner, how did you happen to hear about us?
[42:18] That's the first thing. I mean, and if you recall in your experiences too, right? Many of you, if you've spoken to different companies, the first thing the call rep might do is ask you, how did you hear about us? Right? I'm sure some of you, have you guys, yes? You guys have got that, right? When you talk to them, when you pick up the phone or when you call in, they might ask you, how did you hear about us? Right?
[42:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Offline Conversion Tracking"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de rastreamento de conversão offline, fontes de leads
[42:45] Because that's like a good way. Now, this is a bit deeper, but a more important thing to this is more like offline conversion tracking. You also like for me, like if I'm, if this is my business and you call me, I want to know what my, what marketing efforts are working for me or did you find me organically or did you, your friend refer you to me, right?
[43:08] It helps me gauge what are my more effective methods and I would write them down and note them. This inbound caller came to me through a referral. This person came to me through one of my Facebook ads launched, my campaigns launched two months ago. I want to know that.
[43:28] It is offline conversion tracking, right? Which is something else. It's a different topic, but it's the difference between offline, online. It's online, you know, online conversion tracking. It's like with your computer cookies on your browser or like pixels, right? On your browser.
[43:48] So you click on a site, they pixel you, right? UTM links, unique tracking links, and then cookies, just what's stored in your browser. But then offline is something where like, for example, HubSpot, they let you combine both offline and online to help you see your full buyer's journey. Same as Google Analytics too, right?
[44:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Deep Dive Follow-up Questions"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Técnica de aprofundamento em perguntas, camadas de questionamento
[44:12] So this is really really opening himself up. I need to go a bit deeper. So I'm going to ask this question to get there. We're never going to be able to give you a script or like the perfect B2B question list because it doesn't exist. It's like what Sean and Felix said, you got to flow. You just got to flow.
[44:35] But the benefit of having a class like this is if you can understand the principle, like what people would say, you understand the principle, the why, and you have more tools in your tool belt, more questions to ask, right? You'll be able to pull it out and use it when you need to.
[44:52] It's better to know a million ways and pick one than only having a hundred ways, right? I'd rather have a million and know all of them and know which one to use, right? Like just expand your cup.
[45:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Must-Have, Should-Have, Could-Have Framework"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Framework de priorização de necessidades em três níveis
[45:10] This one is a very interesting question. I like it, but also you got to be careful when using it because like Sean said, if you sound too cocky, no one's going to reply you with this question. It's a very, in a way it's a loaded question and the prospect, they're not stupid, right? They know why you're asking this question, right?
[45:32] Mr. Business Owner, you know, and I'm trying to think of a context because it's a great question when done right. And all these questions are great only when done right.
[45:45] This is really what you're trying to get at is I would say the ideal scenario for this when I've used it in a B2B call was when I've gotten the rapport. I probably wouldn't do this on a discovery call.
[46:00] If I was an AE, AE for sure, build rapport, knows the company, trusts the company a bit more, right? They know why we're speaking today. Then I would probably ask them, so Mr. Business Owner, before we get down in the weeds, I want to actually know what your must-haves, your should-haves, and your could-haves are in regards to your solution, in regards to this solution.
[46:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Must-Have Example"
NÚMEROS: 20% profit margin increase, 6 meses, NPS de 9+
VISUAL: Exemplo prático de categorização de necessidades
[46:30] So at the end of the day, what really needs to happen? That's your must-have, right? And then what's your should-have and nice-to-have, right? That's kind of where I would position that question.
[46:45] But it's a great question because it literally gets the prospect to list out all what's in their head, literally. Like if you execute this right, I will tell you what I really need to have. I will tell you what would be nice to have. And I will tell you what I really don't, I don't really care about.
[47:05] Like I want to get 20, so one, I need to get a 20% profit margin increase. That's a must-have, right? I should be able to do it in six months or less. And a nice-to-have might be, you know, my net promoter score. I want to be able to sell products that will give me an average of nine or above. That's a nice-to-have, right?
[47:28] Then that gives me a list. Boom, right there. You got your whole question, your whole combo list of things to know.
[47:38] SEAN: So I typically use this later in a closing call when I'm getting close to that point where I want to know what is going to take this prospect from being maybe a little bit on the fence and full of objections to we're down to the brass tacks and we know what we need to be able to provide to make this deal happen.
[48:00] So this is a great question when you get close to that later part of the call. And again, more of an AE type call where you're getting closer to the close and you need to know what's going to be the decision makers because this question will give you those decision makers.
[48:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Felix's Alternative Approach"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Método alternativo de fazer a pergunta must/should/could-have
[48:22] FELIX: Yeah, and I'm going to add on top a little bit of my own experience as a product manager before when it comes to these questions is I like to frame it as I'm going to gather the requirements, gather what you need, like what the needs are, and then ask them, are these things a must-have, should-have, or could-have instead of the other way?
[48:48] What are your must-have, should-have, could-have? Because sometimes when you talk to the prospect or when you talk to anyone, they might not know until you ask them, oh, well, you have all these needs. Is it a must-have, should-have, or could-have?
[49:05] That way they would answer those questions much easier because sometimes they might not have a very clear, like is it a must or should or could? Because sometimes they don't think that way. So that way is another way for me to love using these questions on as well.
[49:25] JOEL: Did my slide? Oh, no, my slide's good. I was like, yeah, that's perfect. Yeah, exactly right, Felix. There's a lot of ways to ask it as well. Yeah, you want to demonstrate the question?
[49:38] FELIX: Sure. I mean, let's do it. You could put in an AE closing call if someone wants to play a prospect, Sean or Felix.
[49:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Clarification Framework"
NÚMEROS: 6 necessidades listadas como exemplo
VISUAL: Processo de clarificação de necessidades, lista de 6 itens sendo categorizados
[49:50] JOEL: I think Felix gives you an example just now already. Felix's example, which is a good way of doing it, is really, you have the list of things that the prospect wants or needs, right? And then you clarify with them, Mr. Prospect, you listed out on this call today, you listed out six things that you would like to have.
[50:12] Now from this list of six things, what is really the key? What are the real must-haves from this list? What is like a non-negotiable, right? Another term would be a non-negotiable that needs to happen. Which one out of the six is a non-negotiable, right?
[50:32] And then which one is the nice to have? Like if you don't have it, you know, it's not a deal breaker, but it's just nice to have. I would frame it that way, right? You break it out into those kind of questions.
[50:48] And then which one is really, yeah, it's just, you know, it's nice to have, but really don't need it. Is it nice to have or must have? Sometimes the two is enough that you can really tell, they can just put things in the bucket.
[51:05] And, you know, sometimes you might misunderstand. Like they spend so much time on talking about one need, but that ends up being a nice to have. But they actually have a much bigger problem that you never discussed as a must have that you might need to spend some time on a different call to talk about it.
[51:25] So you need to clarify whatever that needs that you're talking about is a must, should, or could all the time. And the key here, the reason why this is a very powerful question is because like what Felix just said, sometimes on the call, you really did not hit the real pain. You really did not hit the real need.
[51:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Verification Before Moving Forward"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Checkpoint de verificação antes de avançar para fechamento
[51:50] You need to say, hey, Mr. Prospect, we spoke about these things. I just want to confirm that we actually spoke about your must have. Was this in that list? Did we miss something?
[52:05] Because if you miss that and you move on to try to close the deal, it's not going to work. It's not going to happen because they subconsciously, they'll find out later that you never discussed their most important need that they really have to have.
[52:22] So you use this to stop yourself. You stop yourself and you ask, before we move any further, Mr. Prospect, Mr. Business Owner, I want to make sure that these six things, there's nothing else we need to add to this list. They're all your must have, right? And then that's where you go from. You go from that clarification point.
[52:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "DECISION MAKING - Key Question"
NÚMEROS: Categoria #3
VISUAL: Transição para categoria Decision Making, destaque especial
[52:48] Okay. Next category, decision making. This one definitely is different than B2C, I would say. I've almost never asked, I won't say never, but rarely have I asked a HCC prospect, what's your decision making process like, Mr. Prospect? I don't do that, right?
[53:08] I mean, well, true. I mean, sometimes it could be wife, right? It could be the husband, the wife, the spouse, but realistically, I don't ask that to B2C that way. I don't. I really don't. I ask decision maker for B2C in the beginning, right?
[53:28] Is there anyone else that needs to be on this call? Hey, you know, John, before you make a decision like this, is there anyone else that should be involved, right? Before you invest in HCC. That's kind of what I would do.
[53:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Decision Making Process Question"
NÚMEROS: 99% taxa de resposta honesta
VISUAL: Pergunta direta sobre processo de decisão, ênfase em honestidade das respostas
[53:48] But for this, with B2B, it's a really direct question that is separate from B2C. This question is direct. You just throw it out there. I mean, I would throw it out there. Hey, you know, in your company, may I ask, what's your decision making process like?
[54:08] Because 99%, I've never had a business owner or executive or director not answer this question. No, everyone's like to me, very direct, very honest. Yeah, we got a CFO. She has to look at the budget. She has to look at the quote. We got a CEO. He has to review the proposal.
[54:30] Like no one's going to beat around the bush, right? So this is a very great question to ask, and it should be asked, right? Because B2C, like we don't ask that, right? But B2B just has to be asked. Definitely. Definitely has to be asked. Don't miss this question out for sure.
[54:50] SEAN: This is a very important question for the AE because we've already had a couple situations in the past where we didn't make sure we clarified this question, and we actually needed to have two people in the AE's closing call instead of one.
[55:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Multiple Decision Makers Reality"
NÚMEROS: 2+ pessoas frequentemente envolvidas
VISUAL: Representação de múltiplos stakeholders no processo de decisão B2B
[55:10] A lot of B2B decisions aren't made with one person in the room. They're made with multiple people in the room. So definitely something that you need to clarify ahead of time.
[55:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "BUDGET - Investment Question"
NÚMEROS: Categoria #2
VISUAL: Pergunta sobre orçamento, fraseologia específica destacada
[55:28] JOEL: Yeah, definitely. This is Hey, Mr. Prospect, what have you considered is reasonable of an investment for a solution like this? Another variation.
[55:45] This is a one, two, that phrase right is between right and wrong is the difference between green monster and getting the answer you need. And I would say if you execute it properly, you will get a very honest, I will say most of the time you'll get a very honest answer because they, again, like B2B, they really have no reason to lie to you, right?
[56:08] Like, you know, it's very different from B2C. I would say that much for sure. Like also a lot of answers like Don were like, so the truth is, Joel, like we've got a quarterly budget and truthfully right now, we're in May and we're already out of our budget for this quarter, right?
[56:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Budget Constraints Example"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo: Q2 (Maio), orçamento esgotado
VISUAL: Exemplo de resposta sobre limitações orçamentárias trimestrais
[56:32] So really, realistically, I would have another injection of budget funds next quarter, but they're honest. They tell me that they say, you know what, I have our department's out of budget now, but maybe next quarter. Okay, I respect that, right?
[56:50] But again, if you're like the gong AE and you got the balls, which sure, do it, ask them. Is there any chance you could get more budget this quarter? Push for it, right? Push for it, ask it. The worst you can get is a no.
[57:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Challenging Budget Objections"
NÚMEROS: 20% profit increase mencionado como exemplo
VISUAL: Técnica de desafiar limitações orçamentárias com valor
[57:10] Hey, you know, I'm just curious. I know you said you guys ran out of budget this quarter, but if this solution, you know, really got you guys at 20% profit increase, do you think it'd be realistic to just ask your CFO for just some more this quarter? Like just ask for it.
[57:32] The worst they'll say is, no, it's not going to work. We've tried it before. You might get a yeah, you know what? If you could give me that material we talked about, I'll try to present it to my CFO.
[57:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Great AE vs Average AE"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Comparação entre AE mediano e excelente, diferença em push boundaries
[57:50] And that's also, by the way, side note, that's a difference between a great AE and a not great AE, right? You push the boundaries. That's what Desmond's good at. The Sarge is great at that. He doesn't just take a no. We challenge the no. Why is it a no?
[58:10] Sure, you don't have budget now, but could you get the budget? Like it's okay, not in that tonality, but that's my point. Like making the point where a great AE would just go and push buttons. That's what the gong guy did with us too.
[58:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Previous Solutions Question"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Pergunta sobre investimentos anteriores em soluções similares
[58:30] Okay, this is another one, which is, I like it. It's similar to the HCC, B2C question, right? Have you looked at any other programs like this, right? Have you looked at Grant Cardone, Tai Lopez, like, you know, same thing, right?
[58:48] Have you invested in similar solutions? If so, if you have, how much? Have you looked at other CRMs? Oh yeah, we looked at Close.io. We looked at Salesforce. Okay, have you invested in them? No, not yet. Okay, so, you know, I'm the HubSpot guy, right?
[59:08] He asked them. This is a very commonplace question, right? It's key. Why do we want to ask that? Are you talking about this question or the one before? Dan? This one? Oh, I'm going to redirect it back to you.
[59:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Ask About Previous Investments?"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Razões estratégicas para perguntar sobre investimentos anteriores
[59:30] Why did C4 tell us to ask this in HCC? This is an HCC question. Why do you want to know whether the prospect has invested in other programs? Come on, you should know this. We all went through HCC, guys.
[59:48] PARTICIPANTE: I think because if they bought in the past, like they will buy again in the future, right?
[59:55] JOEL: Not only that, not only that. Okay, that's one. But what else? Okay, let me give you the, like, I'm selling HCC to you, right? I'm closing HCC and you, and I want to ask you this, right?
[60:10] Have you invested in other programs? And you tell me, you know, I did Grant Cardone's course. I did Tai's course. I did whoever's course. What's important for that? What's the next question you would ask?
[60:28] PARTICIPANTE: Yeah, exactly. What do you result? What do you result?
[60:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Results & Commitment Questions"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Follow-up crítico sobre resultados de investimentos anteriores
[60:35] JOEL: What did you get from Grant's course? What did you get from Tai's course? Exactly. I want to know that because that showed me your commitment. That showed me how you get results. Same thing here.
[60:50] You invested in HubSpot before? How did it work out for you guys? Did you guys like HubSpot? What did you not like about HubSpot? That's key. What did you like? This is not key. What did you like about HubSpot? Because then you can compare that solution to your solution.
[61:12] Seriously, right? That question, you go follow up. What did you like about them? What did you not like about them? And they'll tell you, well, the customer service, the implementation, you know, that client success guy was nice to me. Like, they'll tell you. They'll tell you the concerns.
[61:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Looking for New Solution?"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Pergunta de aprofundamento sobre motivação para mudança
[61:35] Yeah, and then why are you looking for a new CRM now? I mean, you're a HubSpot. What's the problem? And they'll tell you. The problem is I don't like their, you know, whatever, the customer service guy, right? Whatever, right?
[61:52] So, like, that's the same thing. It's the same concept from B2C.
[62:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Timeline Question - Last Purchase Decision"
NÚMEROS: Exemplos: 6 meses atrás vs 4 anos atrás
VISUAL: Importância do timing da última decisão de compra
[62:02] All right. This one, yes, similar to the previous question. When was the last time you made a purchasing decision for a solution like ours?
[62:15] Because, okay, the why behind this is more about the timeline too. Because it's like, especially though, especially if you're like an SDR doing discovery call, and let's say CRMs, right? Let's say I'm the HubSpot SDR. I call up a company, or sorry, I call up the executive or director, and I'm asking him, hey, so, you know, Mr. Director, when was the last time you guys, your company made a purchasing decision for a CRM?
[62:45] If he or she told me six months ago, we just invested in Salesforce, right? What does that tell you? Versus, oh, yeah, we invested in Salesforce like four years ago. Okay, well, that is very different, right?
[63:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Timeline Analysis"
NÚMEROS: 6 meses vs 4 anos
VISUAL: Comparação de implicações de diferentes timelines de investimento
[63:08] Is it a time for an upgrade? Did you like Salesforce so far, six months? Or why are you with Salesforce for four years? That's a great question to ask. Oh, we love Salesforce. We would never consider moving to any other CRM.
[63:28] All right, and from there, I will ask you, if you're a great SDR, you would push it. You wouldn't just say, oh, they said no, hang up, bye. That's a different topic, by the way. That's a whole different thing about people giving up way too early.
[63:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "No Means Not Right Now"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Mentalidade sobre objeções, "no" como oportunidade de aprofundar
[63:50] When someone says no, they're just like, oh, no. No, a no means, a no literally just means not right now. But let me go dig deeper, right? But that's the why for this question. That's why it's so important.
[64:08] You want to know when they've invested in it, what the outcome was, was it good, was it bad? And would they consider investing in something like this again in the future?
[64:22] PARTICIPANTE: Yeah, same in dating, right?
[64:25] JOEL: Exactly.
[64:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CLOSING - Fit Confirmation Question"
NÚMEROS: Categoria #4
VISUAL: Primeira pergunta de fechamento, técnica de repetição e confirmação
[64:30] All right, I think this is the last two slides. So closing, this is the end. When you want to tie a deal together, right? I found this one a helpful question for sure.
[64:45] Again, this is sort of the same technique in B2C where you sort of, how many of you guys have heard this technique in B2C where you repeat what the customer said on the call, right? You repeat it back to them, right? And then you tie it back to the solution, right?
[65:05] It's literally the same thing. It's pretty much the same idea, right? You repeat based on what we discussed today, ABC123. Do you think our solution is a good fit for your needs? Same concept, same idea, same idea, right?
[65:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2C vs B2B Closing Similarity"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Comparação de técnicas de fechamento B2C e B2B
[65:28] Yeah, maybe it's like, yeah, exactly. You guys know what I'm talking about. It's the same concept, B2C, B2B. Just you phrase it differently, but you get the same result, right?
[65:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Final Critical Question - Next Steps"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Última pergunta crucial sobre passos necessários para fechar o negócio
[65:48] Yep. And then this one is also very key. And this is what I see a lot of people coming from B2C, they always forget to ask this question when it moves to B2B.
[66:05] Because in B2C, we typically don't ask this question. Like when someone's investing in HTC, very rare have I heard a closer ask, what steps do we need to make sure your investment happens? I've almost never heard that.
[66:25] I think I've heard it once or twice by some other closers, but rarely in B2C do people ask that, right? People usually either, there's so many ways of closing, right? They either ask for a credit card or they ask for, where do you wanna go from here, right? What do you wanna do next, right? Are you ready to make this investment?
[66:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Multi-Step B2B Process"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Processo de múltiplas etapas em B2B, discovery → demo → aprovações
[66:50] But in B2B, because it's a multi-step process, that sometimes it's like a discovery call and then now we're in a demo call, right? This is a demo call, okay? So Mr. Business Owner, what steps do we have to take to help make this deal happen?
[67:12] The reason I ask that is because I know this is your making process. They got the CFO. They got a CEO review. They got a board of directors approval. Only then it comes back to this guy.
[67:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mapping the Decision Journey"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Mapeamento do processo de decisão: CFO → CEO → Board → Prospect
[67:30] I want him or her to outline the steps and it's super important. You know why? Because when I wanna do follow-up later, I know exactly where they are in their own decision-making process. As an AE, I need to know where they are. Have you...
[67:32] I want him or her to outline the steps and it's super important. You know why? Because when I wanna do follow-up later, I know exactly where they are in their own decision-making process. As an AE, I need to know where they are. Have you spoken to your CFO yet? Have you gotten the CEO approval yet? Did the board approve it?
[67:55] If I don't know those steps, then when I follow up with them two weeks later, I'm going to be like, hey, Mr. Prospect, just following up. How's it going? And they're like, oh yeah, we're still working on it. Okay, cool. Like that's not a good follow-up, right?
[68:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Follow-up Strategy Based on Process Mapping"
NÚMEROS: 2 semanas mencionadas como timeline de follow-up
VISUAL: Comparação entre follow-up genérico vs follow-up específico baseado em etapas
[68:18] But if I know, okay, so Mr. Prospect, you told me two weeks ago that you need to get CFO approval, then CEO approval, then board approval. Where are you at right now in that process? Oh, I got CFO approval. Great! When are you meeting with the CEO? Oh, next Tuesday.
[68:40] Fantastic. Can I help you prepare anything for that meeting? Do you need any additional materials? Would it help if I joined that meeting? Right? See how different that is? That's the power of asking this question.
[69:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Proactive AE Support"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Lista de ofertas de suporte: materiais adicionais, participação em reuniões, preparação
[69:05] You're not just following up blindly. You're following up strategically because you know the exact steps. And as an AE, you can actually help them move through those steps faster. That's what a great AE does. They don't just sit back and wait. They actively help the prospect navigate their internal approval process.
[69:30] SEAN: And Joel, can I add something to that?
[69:33] JOEL: Yeah, please.
[69:35] SEAN: One of the things that I've found really helpful when you're asking about the steps is also asking about the timeline for each step. So when do you anticipate speaking with your CFO? When does your board meet? Because boards don't meet every day. Sometimes boards meet once a month, once a quarter.
[69:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Timeline Mapping for Each Step"
NÚMEROS: Board meetings: mensal ou trimestral
VISUAL: Timeline visual com marcos de aprovação e frequência de reuniões
[70:00] If you don't know that, you might be following up at completely the wrong time. You might be calling them a week after your last conversation when the board doesn't meet for another three weeks. So you're wasting your time and you're annoying them.
[70:18] But if you know, oh, the board meets on the 15th of next month, then you know, okay, I'm going to follow up on the 16th or 17th to see how that board meeting went. That's strategic follow-up.
[70:35] JOEL: Exactly. That's perfect, Sean. And that's actually a great segue into what I was going to say next, which is, you know, a lot of people, especially coming from B2C, they're used to this mentality of like, I need to close today. I need to close on this call.
[70:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2C vs B2B Closing Timeline Mindset"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Comparação de mentalidade: urgência B2C vs processo B2B de múltiplas etapas
[70:58] And in B2B, that's just not realistic most of the time. Especially if you're selling high-ticket B2B solutions. These deals take time. They take multiple conversations. They take multiple approvals. And that's okay. That's normal.
[71:18] The key is not to get frustrated by that. The key is to understand that's just how B2B works and to work with that process, not against it. You're playing the long game here.
[71:35] FELIX: Yeah, and Joel, one thing I want to add too is that when you're asking about these steps, you also want to understand who the champion is in this process. Like who is actually going to be advocating for your solution internally?
[71:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Identifying the Internal Champion"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Conceito de champion interno, papel no processo de venda
[71:55] Because a lot of times, the person you're talking to might not be the final decision maker, but they might be the person who's going to present your solution to the CFO or to the CEO or to the board. So you need to make sure you're equipping them with everything they need to sell on your behalf when you're not in the room.
[72:18] JOEL: That's a great point, Felix. And that actually ties back to something we talked about earlier with the white papers and the materials, right? Like if this person is your champion and they're going to present to the board, you need to give them a white paper. You need to give them a one-pager. You need to give them something that makes them look good when they present your solution.
[72:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Equipping Your Champion"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Lista de materiais de suporte: white paper, one-pager, case studies, ROI calculators
[72:45] Because if they look good, you look good. If they can confidently present your solution and answer questions, you're way more likely to get that deal. But if you just leave them hanging and they're stumbling through the presentation, you're probably going to lose that deal.
[73:05] So part of asking about the steps is also asking, how can I support you through each of these steps? What do you need from me to make this easier for you?
[73:20] SEAN: And one more thing on this, guys. When you're mapping out these steps, also ask about potential roadblocks. Like, is there anything that could prevent this from moving forward? Is there any concern that the CFO typically has? Is there any objection that usually comes up in board meetings?
[73:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Proactive Objection Handling"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Identificação antecipada de objeções potenciais em cada etapa
[73:45] Because if you can identify those potential objections now, you can address them proactively before they even come up. You can give your champion the answers they need before the questions are even asked. That's next-level selling right there.
[74:05] JOEL: Absolutely. Okay, so let me just recap this section because this is super important. When you're in the closing phase of a B2B deal, you need to ask: What steps do we need to take to make this happen?
[74:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Closing Phase Checklist"
NÚMEROS: 5 pontos principais
VISUAL: Checklist de fechamento com 5 elementos críticos
[74:25] And then you need to:
Map out each step in their approval process
Understand the timeline for each step
Identify who the champion is
Equip your champion with the materials they need
Identify and address potential roadblocks proactively
[74:48] If you do those five things, you're going to close way more B2B deals than someone who's just blindly following up and hoping for the best.
[75:05] All right, I think we're coming up on time here. Let me just check the chat real quick to see if there are any questions before we wrap up.
[75:18] Okay, I see a question from Henry. Henry asks: "What if the prospect says they need to think about it? How do you handle that in B2B versus B2C?"
[75:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Handling 'I Need to Think About It' - B2B Style"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Comparação de abordagens B2C vs B2B para esta objeção
[75:35] Great question, Henry. So in B2C, when someone says "I need to think about it," we typically dig deeper into what they actually need to think about, right? We try to isolate the objection. We might say something like, "I totally understand. Just so I can help you think through this, what specifically do you need to think about?"
[75:58] In B2B, it's similar, but you have to be more respectful of their actual decision-making process. Because sometimes "I need to think about it" genuinely means "I need to run this by my team" or "I need to review this with my CFO."
[76:18] So in B2B, I would say something like: "Absolutely, I completely understand. Just so I can make sure I've given you everything you need to think through this properly, can you walk me through what your thought process looks like? What factors are you going to be considering?"
[76:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B 'Think About It' Response Framework"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Script de resposta profissional e respeitosa
[76:40] And then based on their answer, you can either address concerns right there, or you can schedule a specific follow-up. The key is to not let it be vague. You want to get specific about what they're thinking about and when you'll reconnect.
[77:00] SEAN: And Joel, I would add to that - in B2B, you also want to ask: "Who else needs to be involved in this thinking process?" Because a lot of times, "I need to think about it" really means "I need to talk to other people about it."
[77:18] And if that's the case, you might say: "Would it be helpful if I joined that conversation? Or would you like me to prepare some materials that you can share with those stakeholders?" You're making it easier for them to move forward.
[77:35] JOEL: Perfect. Exactly right, Sean. Okay, another question from the chat. This one's from Shang: "How long should a typical discovery call be in B2B versus a closing call?"
[77:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Call Duration Guidelines"
NÚMEROS: Discovery: 15-30 min, Closing: 45-60 min
VISUAL: Comparação de duração típica de diferentes tipos de calls
[77:55] Good question, Shang. So for a discovery call, you're typically looking at 15 to 30 minutes. You're just trying to qualify them, understand their needs, and see if there's a fit. You're not trying to close them on a discovery call.
[78:15] For a closing call or what we call an AE call, you're looking at 45 minutes to an hour, sometimes longer depending on the complexity of the solution. Because you need time to really dig into their needs, present your solution, handle objections, and work towards a close or next steps.
[78:38] But here's the thing - these are guidelines, not rules. Some discovery calls might be 10 minutes if you quickly realize there's no fit. Some closing calls might be 90 minutes if it's a complex enterprise deal. You have to read the room and adjust accordingly.
[78:58] FELIX: Yeah, and one thing I've noticed too is that in B2B, business owners and executives really appreciate when you respect their time. So if you told them the call would be 30 minutes, you better wrap it up in 30 minutes or ask for permission to go over.
[79:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Time Respect = Professional Respect"
NÚMEROS: 30 minutos usado como exemplo
VISUAL: Importância de respeitar o tempo prometido
[79:20] Don't just keep talking because you haven't closed yet. If you need more time, say: "Hey, I know we're coming up on our 30-minute mark. I have a few more things I'd love to cover. Do you have another 15 minutes, or should we schedule a follow-up?"
[79:38] That shows you respect their time and you're professional. And that goes a long way in B2B.
[79:48] JOEL: Great point, Felix. Okay, I see another question here. This is from Patrick: "What's the difference between an SDR and an AE? Can you clarify those roles?"
[80:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SDR vs AE - Role Definitions"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado das responsabilidades de cada função
[80:08] Absolutely, Patrick. Great question. So SDR stands for Sales Development Representative. The SDR's job is to qualify leads and book appointments. They're doing discovery calls. They're asking qualifying questions. They're determining if there's a fit. But they're not closing deals.
[80:30] If the SDR determines there's a good fit, they pass that qualified lead to an AE, which stands for Account Executive. The AE's job is to close the deal. They do the demo calls, the closing calls, the negotiation, and they get the contract signed.
[80:50] So think of it like this: SDR is top of funnel, AE is bottom of funnel. SDR is qualification, AE is closing. In some organizations, one person does both roles, but in more mature sales organizations, these are separate roles because they require different skill sets.
[81:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SDR to AE Handoff Process"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Funil de vendas mostrando transição de SDR para AE
[81:15] SEAN: And to add to that, in our B2B division here, we have both SDRs and AEs. The SDR does the initial outreach, qualifies the lead, and if there's a good fit, they book a call with an AE. The AE then takes over and works to close the deal.
[81:35] This allows the SDR to focus on volume - talking to lots of prospects, qualifying them quickly. And it allows the AE to focus on quality - spending more time with fewer prospects to actually close deals.
[81:52] JOEL: Perfect. Okay, let me see if there are any other questions before we wrap up here.
[82:00] I see one from Chris: "What CRM do you recommend for B2B sales?"
[82:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CRM Recommendations for B2B"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Logos ou comparação de diferentes CRMs
[82:10] JOEL: Good question, Chris. So we use HubSpot in our organization. HubSpot is great for B2B because it has really good features for tracking your pipeline, automating follow-ups, and managing multiple stakeholders in a deal.
[82:30] Other popular options are Salesforce, which is more robust but also more complex and expensive. There's also Close.io, which is simpler and good for smaller teams. And Pipedrive is another option that's user-friendly.
[82:48] Honestly, the best CRM is the one you'll actually use consistently. Don't get caught up in having the "perfect" CRM. Pick one that fits your budget and your team's needs, and then use it religiously.
[83:05] SEAN: Yeah, and I would add - the most important thing about a CRM in B2B is that you're tracking all your activities and all your touchpoints with each prospect. Because B2B deals have long sales cycles with multiple conversations, you need to be able to look back and see the full history of your interactions.
[83:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CRM Essential Features for B2B"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Lista de features críticas: pipeline tracking, activity logging, stakeholder management
[83:30] If you're not logging your calls, your emails, your meetings in your CRM, you're going to lose track of where each deal is. And that's a recipe for losing deals.
[83:45] JOEL: Absolutely. Okay, I think we have time for maybe one or two more questions. Let me check the chat.
[83:55] Okay, I see one from Dan: "What are the biggest mistakes you see people make when transitioning from B2C to B2B?"
[84:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Top 5 B2C to B2B Transition Mistakes"
NÚMEROS: 5 erros principais
VISUAL: Lista numerada de erros comuns
[84:10] JOEL: Oh man, that's a great question, Dan. I could talk about this for an hour, but let me give you the top five mistakes I see:
[84:20] Number one: Being too casual in their language and tonality. In B2C, you can be more personable and emotional. In B2B, you need to be more professional and business-focused. You can't talk to a CFO the same way you talk to someone buying a personal development course.
[84:42] Number two: Trying to close too fast. In B2C, especially with HTC, we're often closing on the first call. In B2B, that's rare. You need to be patient and work through the process.
[85:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mistake #1 & #2"
NÚMEROS: 2 de 5
VISUAL: Primeiro e segundo erro destacados com exemplos
[85:02] Number three: Not understanding the different types of revenue and financial metrics. Like we covered earlier, you need to speak the language of business. If you can't talk about MRR, ARR, profit margins, etc., you're going to lose credibility fast.
[85:22] Number four: Not mapping out the decision-making process. In B2C, it's usually one person making the decision. In B2B, there are multiple stakeholders. If you don't understand who all needs to be involved, you're going to get blindsided.
[85:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mistake #3 & #4"
NÚMEROS: 3 e 4 de 5
VISUAL: Terceiro e quarto erro com ícones financeiros e stakeholders
[85:42] And number five: Not doing enough research before the call. In B2C, you can often go into a call knowing very little about the person. In B2B, you need to research the company, understand their industry, know their competitors, check out their website, their LinkedIn. If you go in blind, you're going to look unprofessional.
[86:08] FELIX: And Joel, I would add a number six if you don't mind - not asking enough questions. In B2C, you're often doing more telling than asking. In B2B, it should be the opposite. You should be asking way more questions than you're talking. Because you need to understand their business deeply before you can position your solution properly.
[86:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mistake #5 & Bonus #6"
NÚMEROS: 5 e 6 (bônus)
VISUAL: Quinto erro e erro bônus adicionado por Felix
[86:35] JOEL: Great addition, Felix. That's absolutely right. The ratio in B2B should be like 70% listening, 30% talking. Whereas in B2C it might be more 50/50 or even 60/40 the other way.
[86:52] Okay, I think we're coming up on our time here. Let me just do a quick recap of everything we covered today, and then we'll wrap up.
[87:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Session Recap - What We Covered"
NÚMEROS: 3 seções principais
VISUAL: Resumo visual das três partes principais da aula
[87:08] So today we covered three main things:
[87:12] First, we went through B2B sales terminology and business acumen. We talked about things like MQLs, SQLs, white papers, different types of revenue like MRR, ARR, ACV. We talked about KPIs, NPS, margins, all that business language that you need to know to sound credible in B2B conversations.
[87:35] Second, we talked about posturing, framing, and confidence. We did that role-play with Sean and Felix to show you what proper B2B tonality sounds like. We talked about how to schedule appointments directly rather than sending booking links. We talked about how to get commitment from prospects.
[87:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Key Takeaway #1 & #2"
NÚMEROS: 2 de 3 takeaways
VISUAL: Primeiro e segundo ponto principal com ícones representativos
[88:00] And third, we went through crucial B2B questions to ask, organized into four categories: Needs, Budget, Decision Making, and Closing. We talked about questions like "How did you hear about us?", "What are your must-haves, should-haves, and could-haves?", "What's your decision-making process?", "What have you considered reasonable for an investment?", and "What steps do we need to take to make this happen?"
[88:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Key Takeaway #3 - Question Framework"
NÚMEROS: 4 categorias de perguntas
VISUAL: Framework completo de perguntas em 4 blocos
[88:35] Now, like I said at the beginning, this is a foundation class. This is not everything you need to know about B2B sales. But if you take what we covered today and you actually implement it, you're going to be way ahead of most people who are trying to do B2B sales.
[88:55] The key is to practice. Use these terms in conversation. Practice your tonality. Ask these questions on your calls. And over time, it's going to become natural. You're going to develop that business acumen that makes you credible in B2B conversations.
[89:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Next Steps & Resources"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Lista de próximos passos e recursos disponíveis
[89:18] Now, for those of you who are interested in joining our B2B sales division, here's what you need to do next:
[89:25] First, review the recording of this class. Take notes. Make sure you understand all the terminology we covered.
[89:35] Second, practice. Role-play with a friend or family member. Practice using this business language. Practice your tonality. Get comfortable with it.
[89:48] Third, if you're serious about doing B2B sales with us, reach out to Sean or Felix. They can walk you through the next steps, what positions are available, what the requirements are, all of that.
[90:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Division - How to Get Involved"
NÚMEROS: 3 passos
VISUAL: Processo de três etapas para se juntar à divisão B2B
[90:08] SEAN: Yeah, and just to add to that - we're actively looking for both SDRs and AEs right now. If you have B2C closing experience and you're willing to learn and adapt to B2B, we want to talk to you.
[90:25] The B2B division is growing fast, and there's a lot of opportunity here. But you need to be coachable, you need to be willing to learn this new skill set, and you need to be committed to putting in the work.
[90:42] FELIX: And from a support perspective, we have a lot of resources available to help you succeed. We have scripts, we have email templates, we have CRM training, we have ongoing coaching. So if you're willing to do the work, we're going to support you every step of the way.
[91:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Support & Resources Available"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Lista de recursos de suporte: scripts, templates, CRM training, coaching
[91:05] JOEL: Awesome. Thank you, Sean and Felix, for being here and adding so much value to this class.
[91:12] All right, everyone. I think that's going to wrap it up for today. Thank you all so much for being here, for being engaged, for asking great questions. I hope you got a ton of value from this.
[91:25] Remember, B2B sales is different from B2C, but it's not harder. It's just different. And if you're willing to learn the language, develop the right posture, and ask the right questions, you can absolutely crush it in B2B.
[91:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Final Thoughts - B2B vs B2C"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Mensagem motivacional sobre diferença vs dificuldade
[91:45] So take what you learned today, go practice, and let's close some B2B deals. All right, everyone. Have a great rest of your day. We'll see you in the next training.
[91:58] MULTIPLE VOICES: Thank you! Thanks, Joel! Thanks, Sean! Thanks, Felix!
[92:05] JOEL: All right, bye everyone!
[92:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Thank You - B2B Sales Foundation Class"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Slide de encerramento com informações de contato e próximos passos
[92:10] [FIM DA GRAVAÇÃO]
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO (CONTINUAÇÃO E CONSOLIDAÇÃO FINAL)
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS ADICIONAIS:
15-30 minutos: duração típica de discovery call
45-60 minutos: duração típica de closing call (AE)
90 minutos: possível duração para deals enterprise complexos
70% listening / 30% talking: ratio ideal em B2B
50/50 ou 60/40: ratio típico em B2C
2 semanas: exemplo de timeline de follow-up
15th do mês: exemplo de data de reunião de board
6 erros principais: identificados na transição B2C para B2B
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS ADICIONAIS:
Follow-up Estratégico:
Mapeamento completo do processo de aprovação
Timeline específica para cada etapa
Follow-up baseado em marcos, não em tempo arbitrário
Suporte proativo em cada etapa do processo
Champion Management:
Identificação do champion interno
Equipar o champion com materiais (white papers, one-pagers, case studies)
Preparação para apresentações internas
Antecipação de objeções em cada nível de aprovação
Objeção "Need to Think About It":
Framework de resposta respeitosa mas específica
Identificação de stakeholders adicionais
Oferta de participação em conversas internas
Agendamento de follow-up específico, não vago
SDR vs AE Structure:
SDR: qualificação, discovery, booking (top of funnel)
AE: demo, closing, negotiation (bottom of funnel)
Handoff process estruturado entre SDR e AE
Separação permite foco em volume (SDR) vs qualidade (AE)
CRM Best Practices:
HubSpot recomendado (usado pela organização)
Alternativas: Salesforce, Close.io, Pipedrive
Logging obrigatório de todas as interações
Tracking de múltiplos stakeholders
Histórico completo para long sales cycles
ERROS COMUNS NA TRANSIÇÃO B2C → B2B:
Linguagem muito casual - Falta de profissionalismo e business acumen
Tentar fechar muito rápido - Não respeitar o processo de múltiplas etapas
Não entender métricas financeiras - Perda de credibilidade com falta de conhecimento de MRR, ARR, margens
Não mapear processo de decisão - Ser pego de surpresa por stakeholders desconhecidos
Research insuficiente - Entrar em calls sem conhecer a empresa, indústria, competidores
Não fazer perguntas suficientes - Falar demais, ouvir de menos (ratio invertido)
ESTRUTURA ORGANIZACIONAL:
Divisão B2B da Dan Lok Organization:
Divisões ativas: SDR e AE positions
Requisitos: experiência B2C closing, coachability, commitment
Suporte disponível: scripts, email templates, CRM training, coaching contínuo
Crescimento rápido com oportunidades de expansão
Contatos para Ingresso:
Sean (B2B Sales Division Manager)
Felix (Sales Support Manager)
Processo de Onboarding:
Revisar gravação da aula
Tomar notas e estudar terminologia
Praticar role-plays e tonalidade
Entrar em contato com Sean ou Felix
Discussão sobre posições disponíveis e próximos passos
MINDSET FINAL:
Filosofia Core:
"B2B não é mais difícil que B2C, é apenas diferente"
Long game vs quick close
Processo sobre urgência
Profissionalismo sobre personalização
Perguntas sobre afirmações
Suporte ao champion sobre venda direta
Princípios de Sucesso:
Desenvolver business acumen naturalmente (não forçado)
Respeitar processos de decisão corporativos
Equipar champions internos para sucesso
Follow-up estratégico baseado em marcos
Paciência com sales cycles longos
Mapeamento completo antes de tentar fechar
RECURSOS DE APRENDIZADO:
Mencionados na Aula:
Ultra UHTC (módulo 2 - B2B content)
OKR book por John Doerr
Gravação desta foundation class
Scripts e templates da divisão B2B
CRM training materials
Ongoing coaching sessions
Próximas Etapas de Desenvolvimento:
Module 2 do Ultra UHTC para B2B aprofundado
Role-play practice contínua
Real-world application com suporte
Feedback loops com Sean e Felix
DURAÇÃO TOTAL: ~92 minutos (1h32min)
FORMATO: Webinar educacional ao vivo com apresentação de slides, role-play demonstrativo, Q&A interativo, e participação de 3 instrutores principais
PÚBLICO-ALVO: IC members, HTC family members, CITs (Closers in Training) interessados em transição para B2B sales
OBJETIVO PRINCIPAL: Fornecer fundação sólida em B2B sales para permitir sucesso imediato na divisão B2B sem esperar conclusão completa do Ultra UHTC
RESULTADO ESPERADO: Participantes equipados com vocabulário, postura, e framework de perguntas necessários para iniciar carreira em B2B sales com confiança e credibilidade profissional
